html:
Canto VI

Vasco Da Gama departeth from Melinde; and, while he voyageth prosperously, Bacchus descendeth to the sea: Description of Neptune‚Äôs Palace: The same (Bacchus) convoketh the Sea-gods and persuadeth them to destroy the Navigators: Meanwhile Velloso entertaineth his mates with the tale of the ‚ÄúTwelve of England‚Äù: An horrible storm ariseth: It is calmed by Venus and her Nymphs: At length they arrive in calm weather at Calecut, the last and longed-for bourne of this navigation.

Illustrious Gama from Melind√© saileth, (1‚ÅÝ‚Äì‚ÅÝ5)
With native Pilots and fresh nutriment;
Descends Lyaeus seaward; Neptune haileth (6‚ÅÝ‚Äì‚ÅÝ37)
The Gods who rule the humid element:
Velloso, giving praise and honour, telleth (38‚ÅÝ‚Äì‚ÅÝ69)
Of th‚Äô ‚ÄúEnglish Twelve‚Äù the Tale of Tournament:
Succoureth Venus her long suff‚Äôering Fleet (70‚ÅÝ‚Äì‚ÅÝ91)
And thus the wisht-for strand of Inde they greet. (92‚ÅÝ‚Äì‚ÅÝend)

1

Scant could devise how best to entertain
the pagan King our Voyagers renown‚Äôd,
firm friendship of the Christian King to gain
and folk so puissant proved, so faithful found:
Grieveth him greatly, that his rule and reign
be placed so distant from Europa‚Äôs bound
by lot, nor let him neighbour that abode
where opened Hercules the broad sea-road.

2

With games and dances, gentle, honest play
e‚Äôen as accorded with Melindan style,
and fishing frolicks, like the Lageian gay134
delighted Anthony with gladde‚Äôning guile,
rejoiced that famous Sovran every day,
the Lusitanian host to feast and fill
with banquets rich, rare meats and unknown dishes
of fruit and flesh, of birds, and beasts, and fishes.

3

But when the Captain saw him still detain√®d
far more than seem√®d meet, while the fresh breeze
to sail inviteth; and he had obtain√®d
the Negro Pilots and the new supplies;
no longer list he tarry; for remain√®d
long paths to plow through salt and silvern seas;
To the good Pagan bids he warm adieu,
who prays their friendship may be long and true.

4

He prayeth, eke, that Hythe shall ever be
the place where all the Fleets may rest and bait;
for nothing better now desireth he,
than for such Barons to quit reign and state:
Eke, that ere light of Life his body flee
he will on opportunity await
his days to peril and his crown to waive,
for King so kingly and for Braves so brave.

5

Response in sim‚Äôilar speech to such discourse
the Captain gave, and loosing canvas sail√®d,
straight for Auroran regions shaping course,
where his long seeking still so scant avail√®d.
No more his Guide and Pilot had recourse
to fraud and falsehood, nay, he never fail√®d
in his sure seamanship; so sped they o‚Äôer
securer seas than those they sail‚Äôd before.

6

They fought the restless floods that front the Morn
now ent‚Äôering Indic Ocean, and descried
Sol‚Äôs chambers, where the burning God is born;
and ev‚Äôery wish was well-nigh satisfied.
But now that ill Thyoneus‚Äô soul of scorn,
mourning the mighty meeds of power and pride
that Lusian valour wendeth dight to win,
burns and blasphemes with madding rage insane.

7

He saw the potent hosts of Heav‚Äôen prepare
in Lisbon town a novel Rome t‚Äô instal:
Nor aught can alter; such high fortunes are
ruled by the dreadful Pow‚Äôer that ruleth all.
In fine he flies Olympus in despair,
to find on earth new mode remedial:
He thrids the humid Reign and seeks his court
who gained the Gov‚Äôernance of the Seas by sort.

8

Deep in the lowest depths of the profound
and lofty Caves, where surges slumb‚Äôering lie;
there, whence the billows sally furibund
when to fierce winds the fiercer waves reply
bides Neptune, and abide their Lord around
Ner‚Äôeids, and many a sea-born Deity,
where fit for cities leave the waves a plain
dry for the Godheads governing the Main.

9

Discover th‚Äô undiscovered depths of sea
Courts strewn with gravels of fine silver hoar;
and lofty turrets crown that Ocean-lea,
crystalline masses of diaph‚Äôanous ore:
However near the curious eye may be,
so much its judgment shall be less secure
an it be crystal, or the diamant-stone
that doth such clearness and such radiance own.

10

The gates of purest gold, where lies inlaid
rich seed of pearl that in the sea-shell breedeth,
with rarest shapes of sculpture are portray‚Äôd
whereon hot Bacchus pleas√®d glances feedeth:
There ‚Äômid the foremost, limned in light and shade,
old Chaos‚Äô face confus‚Äôd the stranger readeth:
the fourfold El‚Äôements eke he sees translate,
each in his several office and estate.

11

There Fire sublimely held supremest height,
who by no grosser substance was sustain√®d;
lending to living things his life and light,
since by Prometheus stolen and detain√®d.
Behind him, standing high ‚Äôyond mortal sight,
invis‚Äôible Air a lower place maintain√®d‚ÅÝ‚Äî
Aether, which conquered ne by Heat ne Cold,
ne‚Äôer suff‚Äôereth Earth a vacuous space t‚Äô enfold.

12

There deckt with mount and boscage Terra stood
Yclad in grass, shrub, tree of blossom‚Äôd head;
affording life, affording divers food
to ev‚Äôery breathing thing her surface bred:
The glassy figure, eke, ensculptur‚Äôed stood
of Water, veining Earth and interspread,
creating fishes in their varied norm,
and by her humour holding all in form.

13

Carved on another panel showed the fight,
waged by the Gods against the Giantry;
Typhoeus lies ‚Äôneath Aetna‚Äôs serried height,
far flashing crepitant artillery:
There sculptured cometh gravid Earth to smite
Neptune, when taught the salvage Man t‚Äô apply
his gift, the Courser, and to worlds first shown
the peaceful Olive-tree, Minerva‚Äôs boon.

14

With scanty tardance vext Lyaeus eyed
these varied marvels: Soon he past the gate
of Neptune‚Äôs Palace, who had thither hied
the God‚Äôs expected visit to await:
Him at the threshold greets he, ‚Äôcompanied
by Nymphs, who marvel at the freak of Fate
to see, attempting such unus√®d road,
the Wine-god seek the Water-god‚Äôs abode.

15

‚ÄúO Neptune!‚Äù cri√®d he, ‚ÄúRegard not strange
that Bacchus comes a guest within thy Reign;
even we highest pow‚Äôers who reck no change
are prone to suffer Fortune‚Äôs fell disdain:
Summon, I pray, the Gods who Ocean range
ere say I more, if more to hear thou deign;
they shall behold what ills the Gods befall‚ÅÝ‚Äî
all hear what evils overhang us all.‚Äù

16

Already Neptune, deeming worth his heed
a case so novel, sends in hottest haste,
Triton to call the cold Sea-gods with speed,
that govern Ocean‚Äôs breadth from east to west:
Triton‚ÅÝ‚Äîthat boasts him of the Sea-king‚Äôs seed,
who had the reverend nymph, Salatia, prest‚ÅÝ‚Äî
was a tall, huge-limb‚Äôd Carle, young, swart of hue,
his Father‚Äôs trumpet and his courier too.

17

The feltred beard, and matted locks that fell
adown his head and o‚Äôer his shoulders strown,
were water-pregnant weeds, and seemed it well
no soft‚Äôening comb had e‚Äôer their tangles known:
Nor lacketh jet-black fringe of mussel-shell,
pendent from points where mingled growths are grown:
For cap and cowl upon his head he wore
the crusty spoils erst a huge lobster bore.

18

Naked his body, and of cloth are clear
his loins, to swim without impediment;
yet pygmy sea-things clothe with sea-born gear
his limbs, in hundred hundreds spread and sprent;
with shrimps, and crabs, and many such small deer
which from cool Phoebe take their increment;
oysters, and moss-fouled mussels, while each rib
glistens with periwinkles glazed and glib.

19

His Conch, that mighty writh√®d shell, in hand
he bore, and forceful blew with draughty throat;
whose harsh canorous voice, at his command,
heard ev‚Äôery Ocean, ech‚Äôoing far the note:
Now by his summons warned, the god-like band
straight for the Palace left their seats, and sought
the Deity who reared Dardania‚Äôs wall,
by Grecian fury doomed anon to fall.

20

Came Father Ocean, whom accompanied
the sons and daughters gotten in the Main:
Comes Nereus, who led Doris for a bride,
she who replenisht with her Nymphs his Reign:
And, eke, prophetick Proteus thither hied,
leaving his herd to browse the bitter plain:
He came, that wizard; yet right well knew he
what Father Bacchus wanted of the Sea.

21

Came from another quarter Neptune‚Äôs fere,
begot by Coelus, borne by Vesta‚Äôs womb,
of gesture grave yet gay, fair sans compeer,
the wond‚Äôering waves were blandisht by her bloom:
A light Cym√°r of costly weft her wear,
subtle as though ‚Äôtwere wove in airy loom,
that bared the crystal charms to longing eyne‚ÅÝ‚Äî
charms ne‚Äôer create in jealous shade to pine:

22

And Amphitrit√©, bright as flow‚Äôers in spring,
in such conjuncture could not stay away;
bringing the Dolphin, who her heart did bring
her kingly lover‚Äôs wish and will t‚Äô obey;
with glorious orbs that conquer ev‚Äôerything,
and steal his splendours from the Lord of Day:
Hand clasping hand the coupled Consorts trod
the sister spouses of the two-wived God.

23

She, who from furious Athamas of yore
a fugitive, uprose to god-degree,
her son, a lovely youngling, with her bore,135
fated to sit in Heaven‚Äôs consistory:
They linger sporting on the pebbly shore
with pearly conchlets, which the briny sea
aye breeds, and now he stays his sport, and rests
pillow‚Äôd on Panope‚Äôs delicious breasts.

24


And eke the God,136 once made in mould of man,
who by the magick simples‚Äô potent spell
chang√®d to fish, and from such chance began
a thing of time ‚Äômid timeless Gods to dwell,
came still bewailing tricksy Fortune‚Äôs ban,
which the fair maid by Circe‚Äôs spite befell,
Scylla he lov√®d as by her belov√®d;
for love pervert pure hate hath often prov√®d.

25

And now the Godheads all in Council meet
amid the vasty Hall, superb, divine;
Goddesses seated on rich dais seat
Gods throned on tall estrados crystalline;
when rose their awful Host his guests to greet
who by the Theban sat on level line:
Fumeth the Palace with the rich sea-mass137
Araby‚Äôs odours never shall surpass.

26

At length, when tumult sinks to stilly rest,
and when the De‚Äôities all their greetings close,
to them Thy√≥neus opes his hidden breast,
and the sad secret of his torment shows:
A shade of sadness marks his look and gest,
as though deprest by sense of ‚Äôduring woes,
resolved with alien steel alone to slay
right soon the Lusus men, he ‚Äôgan to say:‚ÅÝ‚Äî

27

‚ÄúPrince! who by birthright holdest high command
o‚Äôer the proud seas that sweep from Pole to Pole;
thou who dost curb the den‚Äôizens of the land
that none o‚Äôerpass his term and certain goal:
And, Father Ocean! thou whose ‚Äôcircling band
around the glob√®d universe doth roll,
permitting only by thy just decree
each in due bounds to flourish, Earth and Sea:

28

‚ÄúAnd, eke, ye Water-gods, who ne‚Äôer endure
aught of injurious in your vast domain,
sans meetest chastisement condign and sure,
dealt to the worms who overrun your reign:
Why dwell ye reckless thus, how rest secure?
Who to such softness had the power to train
your hearts, with reason hardened to behold
this race of mortals weak withal so bold?

29

‚ÄúYe saw the wondrous insolent extremes
that dared the heavenly heights in arms to scale:
Ye saw that wildest fantasy that dreams
of conquering Ocean-tide with oar and sail:
Ye saw, and every day we see, meseems,
such braves, such insults that, if these prevail,
full soon, I fear, of sea and sky to find
Mankind the godheads, Gods the humankind.

30

‚ÄúYou see that now this weak ephemeral brood,
who from a Vassal mine hath taken name,
with sprite high-flown, and heart of proudest mood,
you, me and all the world would tempt and tame:
You see how freely they defy your Flood,
a doughtier deed than Rome‚Äôs high race could claim:
You see they seek to ‚Äôspy your whole domain,
to break the very statutes of your Reign.

31

‚ÄúI saw how ‚Äôgainst the Minyae,138 first to find
the path that passeth through your realm, the wave,
much-injured Boreas, with his brother-wind
Aquilo and their peers, did rage and rave.
If to th‚Äô adventurous mortals who design‚Äôd
such wrongs the Winds appaid the boast and brave,
ye, who have higher right these wrongs to pay,
what wait ye? Doom of justice why delay?

32

‚ÄúNor will I, Gods! consent, so should you trow
pure love of you from Heaven hath brought me down;
not thus your suff‚Äôering feel I and your woe,
what wrongs I now resent are all mine own;
since the high honours, as your Godships know,
I won on earth, when fell by me o‚Äôerthrown
Inde‚Äôs wealthy Reign, of Morning-lond the grace,
I see abated by this little race:

33

‚ÄúFor our all-Sovran Sire and eke the Fates
who rule this nether world as best they wot,
resolve with Fame which ne‚Äôer on man awaits,
to make th‚Äô abysmal sea these Barons‚Äô lot:
Hence shall you view, O Gods! their human hates
teach god to work god wrong: Ah! see ye not
of note and worth we have the smallest boast
whose value Reason valueth the most.

34

‚ÄúWherefore Olympus‚Äô height I now have fled,
to seek heart-salving balm for sore despair;
eke would I find, if rank thus forfeit√®d
in Heav‚Äôen, your Waters still to honour care.‚Äù
More would he say, but nothing more he said,
for tears, already trickling pair by pair,
leapt from his brimming lids, and as they came
the Gods of Water felt their sprites aflame.

35

The rage which sudden fired their hearts divine,
and roused to such display each vengeful soul,
suffered not counsel to contain design,
nor discount brook√®d, nor endured control:
Now to great Aeolus they send a sign,
as ‚Äôtwere from Neptune, bidding him enroll
contrary Winds of wildest phrenesy,
and of all vent‚Äôurous sails sweep clean the sea.

36

Proteus the first and foremost there desir√®d
to speak his feelings as he felt him bound;
the general Conclave deeming him inspir√®d,
by some myst‚Äôerious prophesy profound:
yet was that Company divine so fir√®d
by sudden tumult; brake such storm of sound
that Tethys rising cries indignantly,
‚ÄúWell kens King Neptune what commandeth he.‚Äù

37

Now there superb Hippotades139 gave vent
to furious Winds erst pent in prison-hold;
the while his wilful words fresh fury lent,
against the Lusian Barons brave and bold.
Sudden the summer-vault with clouds was sprent,
for Winds, still growing fierce with rage untold,
gather as on they go fresh might and main,
house, tow‚Äôer, and hillock strewing o‚Äôer the plain.

38

While thus in Council met the Gods‚Äô array
beneath the Seas, before soft breezes float
our joyous weary Ships, and hold their way
o‚Äôer tranquil Ocean on the long new route.
The hour was that when hangs the Lamp of Day
from hemisphere E√∂an most remote:
They of night‚Äôs early watch140 lay down to sleep,
while others waked the second ward to keep.

39

Drows‚Äôiness mastered, all half-numbed and chill
shivered with many a yawn the huddling Crew
beneath the bulging main-sail, cloth√®d ill
to bear the nightly breath that keenly blew;
their eyes, kept open sore against their will,
they rubbed, and stretcht their torpid limbs anew:
To seek a waking-draught the men devise,
spin stories, tell a thousand histories.

40

One ‚Äôgan to say, ‚ÄúWherewith may better we
spur tardy Time who lags so sore and slow,
save with some pretty tale of joyaunce gay
that heavy slumber trouble us no mo?‚Äù
Replied Le‚Äôonardo, truest lover he,
whose firm and constant thought was aye aglow:
‚ÄúWhat tale our tardy breasts may better move
and kill old Time than some fair Lay of Love?‚Äù

41

‚Äú‚Ää‚ÄôTwere not, methinks,‚Äù Velloso said, ‚Äúthing meet
on theme so soft in hours so hard to dwell;
the rough Sea-labours, which do fag the fleet,
Love‚Äôs delicatest fancies rudely quell:
Rather of fervid fight and battle-feat
be now our story, for I see full well,
life is all hardship, and good sooth I wis
more trouble cometh; something tells me this.‚Äù

42

All with his words consenting joint assail
Velloso to recount whate‚Äôer he knew.
‚ÄúI will recount,‚Äù quoth he, ‚Äúnor shall you rail
at aught that seemeth fabulous or new:
And that my hearers learn from this my tale
high proofs of forceful deed to dare and do,
e‚Äôen of my countrymen I‚Äôll say my say;‚ÅÝ‚Äî
the Twelve of England shall adorn my lay.

43

‚ÄúWhen of our Reign the curbing rein so light
John, son of Pedro, held with mod‚Äôerate hand;
and when his Realm had ‚Äôscaped the bane and blight
oft dealt by hate of hostile neighbour-land;
there in great England, where the rain falls white
from Boreal snow-drift, fierce Erinnys plan‚Äôd
to sow the dil‚Äôigent tares of wanton strife,
and make our Lusitania lustre-rife.

44

‚ÄúBetwixt the gentle Dames of th‚Äô English Court,
and high-born Courtier-crowd, one day it came
that horrid Discord showed her dreadful port;
of self-will sprung, or faith in common fame.
The Courtier-throng that lightly loves in sport
and careless mood to bruit the gravest shame,
sware Honour they disprov‚Äôd, and Honesty
in certain Dames, who boasted Dames to be.

45

‚ÄúNay, more, if any Knight uphold as true,
and with his brand and lance the cause defend,
in lists or ras√®d field, the same should rue
foul infamy, or come to cruel end:
The woman-weakness which but little knew,
if e‚Äôer, such foul reproach, and yet which ken‚Äôd
its want of nat‚Äôural force could only crave
their friends to succour and their kin to save.

46

‚ÄúBut as their sland‚Äôerers great and puissant were
throughout the kingdom, none the cause would heed;
nor kith, nor friends, nor fervid lovers dare
support the Dames in darkest hour of need:
Tempting with delicate tear and doleful air
the very Gods to rise in arms, and aid
from Heav‚Äôen, for sake of alabaster brows,
to ducal Lancaster141 the Bevy goes.

47

‚ÄúThis lord was English and in doughty fight
against Castile for Portugale made war,
wherein he proved the noble force and sprite
of his companions, and their fav‚Äôouring star:
Nor less within our realm he saw the might
of Love, whose am‚Äôorous feats as forceful are,
when his fair daughter so the heart did win
of our stout King that chose her for his Queen.

48

‚ÄúHe who in person succour must withhold,
lest fire of civil discord thus be fan‚Äôd,
replied:‚ÅÝ‚Äî‚ÄòWhen I my rights upheld of old
to Spanish kingdom in th‚Äô Iberian land,
I saw in Lusia‚Äôs sons a soul so bold,
such primacy of heart, such open hand,
that they, and only they, I deem, shall dare
with brand and firebrand for your case to care.

49

‚Äú‚Ää‚ÄòAnd, if, aggriev√®d Dames! ye hold it meet
I‚Äôll send my Heralds speaking in your name,
while let your letters, courteous and discreet,
declare your insult, and bewail your shame.
Eke on your side, with pretty phrases sweet,
and soft caresses, let each injured Dame
temper her tears, and venture I to say
you shall strong succour see and steadfast stay.‚Äô

50

‚ÄúThus doth the Duke experienced speak his mind,
and of his bravest friends twelve names he quotes:
That suit‚Äôable Champion be to each assign‚Äôd,
he wills the nam√®d Knights be chose by lots;
because the Dames be twelve; and when they find
which Brave to which Belle-dame his life devotes,
each unto each shall write and claim her rights,
all to their King, the Duke to all the Knights.

51

‚ÄúThe mess‚Äôenger now in Lusia-lond arriveth;
the Court rejoiceth at such novelty:
Our King sublime to ‚Äôlist the foremost striveth,
but suffereth not the kingly dignity:
No courtier but whose valiant sprite aspireth
to volunteer with fervid volunty,
and only he high favour‚Äôd is proclaim√®d
whom for such noble feat the Duke hath nam√®d.

52

‚ÄúThere in the loyal City whence (‚Äôtis said
by olden Fame), arose the name eternal
of Portugalia, a nimble barque he bade
be ‚Äôquipt, who holds the helm of rule internal.
The Twelve in briefest season ready made
arms and accoutrements of use hodiernal;
helms, crests, and mottoes of choice mode they choose
horse, selle, and harness of a thousand hues.

53

‚ÄúNow, when dismiss√®d by their King had been,
sail from the Douro regions famed afar,
the luck-loved Twelve, who did th‚Äô approval win
of England‚Äôs Duke experienced in war.
Amid the dozen was no diff‚Äôerence seen
in chivalry, while skill and strength were par;
then one, Magri√ßo142 hight, and only he
this way addrest the doughty company:‚ÅÝ‚Äî

54

‚Äú‚Ää‚ÄòValiantest comrades! longings manifold
I nurst for many a year the world t‚Äô explore,
Rivers by Tagus nor by Douro roll‚Äôd,
various nations, laws, and varied lore:
And now that matters fit in certain mould
(since Earth of marvels hath extended store),
I would, an leave ye give, alone go round
by land, and meet you upon English ground.

55

‚Äú‚Ää‚ÄòAnd, should I haply ‚Äôcounter let or stay,
from Him who holds of things the ultime line,
and fail to find you on our trysting day,
scant fault to you shall bring default of mine.
You all shall do my duty in the fray;
but, an my prescient sprite the Truth divine,
ne stream, ne mount, ne jealous Fate hath pow‚Äôer
to nill I hail you at th‚Äô appointed hour.‚Äô

56

‚ÄúThus spake Magri√ßo and, his friends embrac√®d,
he fareth forwards when their leave was tane:
In Leon and Castile‚Äôs old realms he trac√®d
sites patrial Mars had granted us to gain:
Navarre and all the dang‚Äôerous heads he fac√®d
of Pyrenee departing Gaul from Spain;
and, seen of France the highest scenes and best,
in Flanders‚Äô grand emporium143 took his rest.

57

‚ÄúThere halting, or by chance or whim‚Äôs command,
for days he tarried, making much delay:
Meanwhile the stout Elev‚Äôen, a glorious band,
plow northern waters scatt‚Äôering freezy spray.
Arrived on stranger England‚Äôs distant strand,
at once to London-town all took the way:
The Duke receives them in his festive hall,
the Dames do service, greeting one and all.

58

‚ÄúNow Time and Tide are ready for the fight
with th‚Äô English Twelve who first afield are shown,
chose by their King, right sure of every Knight:
Helms, crests, greaves, coats, and harnesses they don:
The Dames already deem the fulgent might
of Portugalia‚Äôs Mavors all their own:
In golden owche and rainbow-silks clad
and thousand jewels, sit they gay and glad.

59

‚ÄúBut she, who claim√®d by the chance of lot,
missing Magri√ßo, drest in mourning dyes
sits sad; for she and only she hath not
a knightly champion in this high emprize:
Though our Elev‚Äôen proclaim√®d on the spot,
to England‚Äôs Court, of battle such assize,
that mote the Dames their cause victorious call,
though of their champions two or three may fall.

60

‚ÄúNow in the lofty public Lists convene,
the King of England and his suite and Court:
In threes by threes, and fours by fours are seen
spectators rang√®d by the rule of sort.
From Tage to Bactrus144 ne‚Äôer did Sol, I ween,
flame on such force and fierceness, power and port,
as on those English Twelve, who leave their walls
to front Eleven of our Portingalls.

61

‚ÄúChamping their golden bits, fleckt spumy white,
the chargers cast fierce fiery looks askance:
On arms and armour Phoebus danceth bright
as on dure adamant or crystal glance:
Not less on either side astound the sight
numbers unequal, a quaint dissonance,
to twelve eleven matched: Begins the crowd
to vent its general joyaunce long and loud.

62

‚ÄúAll turn their faces curious to see
where loudest bruit and hottest bate arise:
When lo! a horseman, arm√®d cap-√Ý-pie,
pricks o‚Äôer the plain to claim of war the prize:
Saluting King and Dames, straight rideth he
to his Eleven: ‚ÄôTis the great Magri√ße:
With warmest accolade his friends he haileth,
whom in the battle, cert√®s, ne‚Äôer he faileth.

63

‚ÄúThe Ladye, hearing that the man was there,
who would in combat guard her name and fame,
wends glad the fleece of Helle‚Äôs beast to wear,
which more than Virtue vulgar hearts doth claim:
They cry, ‚ÄòLet go!‚Äô and now the trump‚Äôs shrill blare
fireth the warrior-heart with fiercer flame:

64

‚ÄúThe tramp of destr‚Äôiers riseth with a noise

65

‚ÄúSome sleep to wake no more, in lasting swoon

Horses sans riders here o‚Äôer tilt-yard run,

66

‚ÄúTo waste long words and War‚Äôs extremes to show

67

‚ÄúThe Duke our conqu‚Äôering Twelve forthwith invites
where ring his halls with feast and wassail gay:
Hunters and kitcheners to toil incites
of the Twelve Dames that goodly company;

68

‚ÄúWithal, the great Magri√ßo, men declare,

abroad remain√®d and perform√®d there

And be‚Äôing no carpet-knight, but prompt to dare
what exploits, Mars! thou biddest man to do
He slew a Frank in field; and thus had he

69

‚ÄúOf the stout Twelve another cast his lot

Velloso ceasing here, his mates besought

70

But at this passage when each prickt his ear,

he bade them take in topsails shouting loud
‚ÄúYarely, my lads! look out, the wind increases

71

Scarce were the foresails hurr‚Äôiedly taken in,

‚ÄúFurl!‚Äù cried he, ‚ÄúFurl for life the mainmast-sail!‚Äù

72

Meanwhile the Crew with cries the welkin tore,

‚ÄúHeave!‚Äù roared the Master with a mighty roar,
‚ÄúHeave overboard your all, tog‚Äôether‚Äôs the word!

Hurrieth to ply the pumps the soldier-host,

Such were the gale-gusts, never Tempest blew

‚ÄôMid the great washing waves that greater grew,

75

The sturdy craft that Paul da Gama bears,

76

In air the Ships are thrown with ev‚Äôery throw

77

The Halcyon birds their melancholy wail

78

Ne‚Äôer forged such lightning-bolts of living fire

Never was known the mighty Thunderer‚Äôs ire

79

How many mountains levelled with the lea

Ne‚Äôer reckt those bulky cable-roots to see

80

Da Gama, seeing that so near the scope

By nat‚Äôural Fear confused, and sans a hope

‚ÄúCelestial Guard! divine, angelical

thro‚Äô Erythrean waters cleft in twain:

‚ÄúIf through new perilous paths a way I wore

83

‚ÄúOh happy they whose hap it was to die

Thus he, while battling Winds still fiercer clash√®d,

85

But now the lovely Star145 with sparkling ray,

86

‚ÄúHere, cert√®s, Bacchus‚Äô handwork I descry,‚Äù

87

Garlands she bade them wear of varied hue,

88

And thus it was: For when in Beauty‚Äôs pride

89

‚ÄúThink not, fere Boreas! e‚Äôer ‚Äôtwas thought of mine

Bespake fair Galat√©a in such strain

Thus eke had others equal pow‚Äôer to tame

Now splendid Morning tipt the hills with red

‚ÄúCalecut-land, if aught I see aright!‚Äù

‚ÄúThis is, pardie, the very Land of Inde,

94

Praise to his God he gave, and rightly gave,

95

Amid such fierce extremes of Fear and Pain,

96

Not with the novel viands exquisite,

But by the doughty arm and sword that chase

‚Äômid wrathsome winds and bitter billows thrown,

98

The face enforcing when the cheek would pale

99

Thus wax our mortal wits immortal bright
